coredata.dc:title: Optimization and Comparison of Photovoltaic Parameters of Zinc Oxide (ZnO)/Graphene Oxide (GO) and Zinc Oxide (ZnO)/Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) Hybrid solar cell using Firefly Algorithm for application in Solar Trigeneration System in Commercial Buildings
coredata.dc:creator: Tyagi, Sakshi
Kumar Singh, Pawan
Kumar Tiwari, Arun
Pain, Pritam
coredata.dc:description: Solar cells formed by the combination of organic and inorganic nanoparticle semiconductors are gaining interest in today’s era because of their major features, such as being suitable for scalable solar power conversion and being of low cost to produce desirable photovoltaic devices. This work is an attempt to develop two forms of hybrid solar cells, one by the amalgamation of zinc oxide and carbon quantum dots and second, by incorporating graphene oxide into zinc oxide. In this study, optimization, validation, and comparison of the photovoltaic parameters of the two nanostructured solar cells were attempted using the Artificial Neural Network technique. The ANN was instructed using the Firefly Algorithm. The input parameters are the spectral power density and temperature. The output parameters are the short circuit current (ISC), open-circuit voltage (VOC), fill factor of the cell (FF), and maximum voltage point prediction (VMPP). The results obtained for both the hybrid solar cells were compared and it was noted that the addition of CQDs to ZnO resulted in a considerable increase in the values of ISC, VOC and FF. The output obtained by the ANN trained model was compared with the results obtained from the experimental tests. It is observed that there is considerable agreement between the results obtained from the ANN and experimental values. The optimized values of VOC, ISC, FF, VMPP, Incident Photon to Current Efficiency (IPCE), Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) for ZnO/GO are 0.623 V, 0.546 mA, 62%, 0.509 V, 36.95%, 9.12% respectively. Similarly values of VOC, ISC, FF, VMPP, IPCE, PCE for ZnO/CQDs are 0.636 V, 0.597 mA, 68%, 0.531 V, 40.72%, 10.35% respectively. Therefore, the precise values of all the stated parameters were obtained for both the cells after optimization and hybrid cells made of CQDs proves to be a better candidate. Also, the desirable value of the coefficient of correlation (R) is obtained, approximately close to 1, which means that the fabricated samples are efficient for useful applications. Proper execution of this algorithm on any model of the hybrid solar cell can lead to an evolution in the field of solar cells, resulting in the improvement of efficiency. These optimized cells have been utilized to propose two models of solar trigeneration system used in a commercial building North Service Centre (Olefin building) of Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, West Bengal, India. The trigeneration systems were based on photovoltaic modules, heat pump and photovoltaic-thermal collectors. The objective is to provide enough electricity, domestic hot water, heating and cooling power to meet the typical demand of a single office building. System performance has been predicted and evaluated in the work. Considerations should be made regarding the physical constraints imposed by the environment where the installation has to be performed. The location should be carefully selected to achieve maximum efficiency.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar cells formed by the combination of organic and inorganic nanoparticle semiconductors are gaining interest in today’s era because of their major features, such as being suitable for scalable solar power conversion and being of low cost to produce desirable photovoltaic devices. This work is an attempt to develop two forms of hybrid solar cells, one by the amalgamation of zinc oxide and carbon quantum dots and second, by incorporating graphene oxide into zinc oxide. In this study, optimization, validation, and comparison of the photovoltaic parameters of the two nanostructured solar cells were attempted using the Artificial Neural Network technique. The ANN was instructed using the Firefly Algorithm. The input parameters are the spectral power density and temperature. The output parameters are the short circuit current (ISC), open-circuit voltage (VOC), fill factor of the cell (FF), and maximum voltage point prediction (VMPP). The results obtained for both the hybrid solar cells were compared and it was noted that the addition of CQDs to ZnO resulted in a considerable increase in the values of ISC, VOC and FF. The output obtained by the ANN trained model was compared with the results obtained from the experimental tests. It is observed that there is considerable agreement between the results obtained from the ANN and experimental values. The optimized values of VOC, ISC, FF, VMPP, Incident Photon to Current Efficiency (IPCE), Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) for ZnO/GO are 0.623 V, 0.546 mA, 62%, 0.509 V, 36.95%, 9.12% respectively. Similarly values of VOC, ISC, FF, VMPP, IPCE, PCE for ZnO/CQDs are 0.636 V, 0.597 mA, 68%, 0.531 V, 40.72%, 10.35% respectively. Therefore, the precise values of all the stated parameters were obtained for both the cells after optimization and hybrid cells made of CQDs proves to be a better candidate. Also, the desirable value of the coefficient of correlation (R) is obtained, approximately close to 1, which means that the fabricated samples are efficient for useful applications. Proper execution of this algorithm on any model of the hybrid solar cell can lead to an evolution in the field of solar cells, resulting in the improvement of efficiency. These optimized cells have been utilized to propose two models of solar trigeneration system used in a commercial building North Service Centre (Olefin building) of Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, West Bengal, India. The trigeneration systems were based on photovoltaic modules, heat pump and photovoltaic-thermal collectors. The objective is to provide enough electricity, domestic hot water, heating and cooling power to meet the typical demand of a single office building. System performance has been predicted and evaluated in the work. Considerations should be made regarding the physical constraints imposed by the environment where the installation has to be performed. The location should be carefully selected to achieve maximum efficiency.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of PV cells is the most significant step that has been taken by mankind in the domain of clean and green technology. The generation of clean electricity makes these cells highly demanding and useful. This exclusive invention strides to the cutback in carbon emissions released by a regularly noticeable increase in the utilization of fossil fuels by the economies [1]. Besides the capability to reduce carbon emissions, solar cell technology has attained vital recognition because of its economic interests. Presently, research has concluded the successful evolution and production of hybrid photovoltaic cells. However, it remains controversial whether this invention remains initiatory and the rate of growth in this field is slow and it requires a boost [2].

The primary challenge faced by the entire research community in the development of this technology is the lower efficiency of the cell, which makes it less reliable and creates numerous constraints even after the advantage of substantial power outputs. The idea of hybrid solar cells has furnished a biased solution to this problem, but the efficiency of the same was not entirely desirable. Still, the solar group is functioning endlessly concerning advancement in ANN to enhance the properties of hybrid solar cells. In previous researches, it was reported that the extraction of photovoltaic properties has been done using ANN using different algorithms, but desirable results were not obtained. One of the reasons behind this setback is the weak optimization of the cells and other being an improper selection of organic and inorganic semiconductor pairs, which rendered undesirable results [3,4]. Furthermore, there is a gap between the economic output and the theoretically calculated potential. At the same time, the implementation of modern fabrication techniques has increased the efficiency of solar cells. It is the need of the hour to investigate suitable procedures for improving the results and introducing the best value of possible efficiency ratio in a hybrid solar cell. To address these problems effectively, a novel ANN approach using the firefly algorithm is incorporated in the discipline of solar cells [5]. In this work, two pairs of hybrid solar cells were fabricated. Their PV characteristics were optimized using the ANN-firefly algorithm and compared with the experimental results. The semiconductors used to develop hybrid solar cells are- inorganic semiconductors being Zinc Oxide (ZnO), the organic semiconductor being Graphene Oxide (GO), and Carbon quantum dots (CQDs). ZnO is an excellent n-type material for applications in photovoltaic (PV) devices. It has good electrical, optical, environment-friendly, and structural properties, making it extremely useful. Graphene Oxide (GO), an organic semiconductor, enhances the rate of charge transfer in the device [8]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have proven to be a promising nanocarbon platform in the area of optoelectronics on account of their excellent dispersibility in solvents with high stability and tunable electronic properties [9–11]. The focus of this study lies in the development of an ANN model using training data, which is extracted from numerous iterations of a ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQD hybrid solar cell [6,7]. For training the ANN model, the firefly algorithm was found to give the best values with a negligible error, and the resultant output so obtained was exhibited in this study. The results obtained from the neural network model have been used to describe the parameters of the improved model, which additionally helps to give the IV characteristic curves. The optimized values are compared to the experimental values to find out the degree of compatibility between the two methods of analysis and the results obtained are desirable. Please refer to Fig. 1 which shows the flow chart explanation of the objective.

ANN has been successfully applied to study different parameters of solar cells; The focus is on the prediction of the maximum power point which is considered to be a measure of efficiency, making it applicable for modeling a solar trigeneration unit for a commercial building [12,13]. The study in this discipline is thoroughly concentrated on training numerous neural networks by different methods hence predicting the most acceptable model for a given cell. This work can further be utilized in different topographical areas by considering temperature and spectral power density of the location. Therefore, according to the particular installment site, the required cells can be modeled to meet the demands. In this current scenario of COVID 19, the solar power sector in the world has been impacted adversely due to low availability of cells and modules. To stabilize the sector it is highly recommended to set up more manufacturing firms. Social distancing being the utmost precautious measure should be obeyed by everyone. It leads to high dependency on the simulation and optimization services. This research will help in optimization of the cell performance, reducing operation time and excessive manufacturing cost, also reducing the chances of contracting infection by involvement of less manpower during this crucial period. No research has been performed on these pairs of cells using the above-mentioned optimization technique and the comparison of the experimental and optimized values is the distinct feature in the approach, which altogether brings to the solar community a new scope for future work. Being a diversified area, there are enormous applications of solar energy. One of the applications is in solar trigeneration systems. Solar trigeneration systems are capable of generating electricity, heat and cooling power from solar energy simultaneously with refrigeration unit driven by electricity. The proposed application of this optimized solar cell is in solar trigeneration unit installed at North Service Centre (Olefin building) of Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, West Bengal. The proposed system model combines self governing photovoltaic system, a solar DHW system and a heating, ventilation and air-conditioning (HVAC) system. Additionally, the constructed designs are useful in evaluating two different system performances and hence identifying a better system which is cost efficient, environmental friendly and convenient. We have successfully designed solar modules fabricated from ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs hybrid solar cells and flat plate PV/T collectors. Provisions of electric power to drive cooling equipment and low temperature heat for Domestic Hot Water (DHW) are the essential features of the selected PV/T collector.

The aim of this research is to assess the capability of a solar trigeneration system for the off-grid commercial complex based on PV modules, PV/T collectors and heat pump by following the modeling process. The applied methodology in this article is utilised to design a system which is capable of continuous operation for an off-grid housing and commercial complexes. This research can be further extended to other applications, which may contradict according to the need the suggested technology.

Materials used for the synthesis of Zinc Oxide are Zinc nitrate, sodium hydroxide, and ethanol which were procured from Hemadri chemicals, Mumbai, and were brought in use without further purification. Synthesis of nanoparticles of Zinc oxide was done by the wet chemical method using sodium hydroxide and zinc nitrate precursors. The amount of aqueous ethanol solution of zinc nitrate (Zn(NO3)2·4H2O) taken was 0.5 M which was subjected to constant stirring using a magnetic stirrer. It takes an hour for zinc nitrate to completely dissolve. Likewise, 0.9 M aqueous ethanol solution of potassium hydroxide (KOH) was also prepared with stirring for one hour at a speed of 300–350 RPM. When zinc nitrate completely dissolves, then under high-speed constant stirring 0.9 M KOH aqueous solution was added dropwise for a short period of 45 min, ensuring that the solution touches the walls of the flask. After the total inclusion, the flask walls were sealed to allow the reaction to proceed for 2 hrs. Once the reaction has completed the solution was left undisturbed overnight and then the supernatant solution was then carefully segregated [14]. The remaining solution was centrifuged for 10 min. The precipitate obtained for ZnO NPs were washed with deionized water and ethanol thrice to eliminate the byproducts. The precipitate is then dried at about 60 °C in air. During the drying process, Zn(OH)2 is completely converted to ZnO, Fig. 2 .

Natural graphite flake of size − 325 mesh with a purity of 99.8% was procured from Sigma Aldrich. For the preparation of GO, the modified Hummer method is used [15]. To study in detail, the amount of graphite and NaNO3 used was 5 g and 2.5 g respectively, they were mixed with and 12 ml H3PO4 and 108 ml H2SO4. The solution is stirred in an ice bath for 15 min. To keep the temperature of the mixture below 5 °C, 15 g of KMnO4 was slowly added. Then the suspension was reacted for 2 h in an ice bath. Again, it is stirred for 60 min in a 40 °C water bath. The temperature of the mixture was maintained constant at 98 °C by adding water continuously for 70 min. To increase the volume of suspension up to 500 ml, deionized water was further added. Then after a gap of 15 mins, 15 ml of H2O2 was added. The product so obtained was centrifuged and washed with 5% HCL and deionized water twice. Ultimately, the product was dried at 60 °C, refer Fig. 3 .

Synthesis of Carbon Quantum Dots was done by the facile bottom-up method using a hydrothermal reaction. Fumaronitrile was purchased from (TCI Chemicals India Pvt Limited) was used in its purest form. 10% fumaronitrile aqueous solution was used to conduct hydrothermal reaction by using a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 25 min [16–18]. Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich of 0.2 µm pore size. It is used to filter the above solution. The filtrate so obtained was further purified by continuous centrifugation and re-dispersion in water up to 10 times. The obtained CQDs were then stored in a refrigerator for further use, refer Fig. 4 .

The Indium Tin Oxide (ITO) glass substrate was firstly cleaned with distilled water followed by the application of 2-isopropanol, de-ionized water, and acetone. Now the mixture of P3HT (Mark lisicon SP001): PCBM (Ossila Ltd) was prepared by dissolving them with a molar ratio of 1:0.7 in 0.5 ml chlorobenzene which acts as a solvent. To achieve the homogenous solution, the following blend was stirred at room temperature for a period of 24 h. At a speed of 3000 rpm, this solution was evenly spread on ZnO/GO coated film by spin coating for 30 s. Now to remove the stresses in the morphology of films, the sample was annealed at 90○C for 10 min on a hot plate. The PEDOT: PSS (purchased from Sigma Aldrich) layer was spin-coated on the P3HT/PCBM layer at 4000 rpm for 30 s. Then these films were placed in a copper mask [7,11]. The thermal electrode deposition method was used to deposit the aluminum electrode on the films (work function 4.3 eV) for electrical connections, refer Fig. 5 . The thickness of fabricated cell was 2254 µm and the dimensions were 75 × 25 mm, shown in Fig. 7(a).

The ITO-coated glass substrates were cleaned in a sonication bath with deionized water, acetone, and methanol for 3 min per cleaning agent. For the preparation of the ZnO layer, 6 vol% ethanolamine was mixed with 0.1 M nickel acetate (purchased from Sigma-Aldrich) in ethanol. The ZnO precursor solution was added to the synthesized dots. Then at a speed of 3,000 rpm, ZnO/CQD solution was spin-coated onto treated ITO glass at for 40 s. Also, thermal-annealing was performed at 350 ℃ for about 30 min in air. Now the films were placed in a copper mask, Fig. 6
. Aluminum electrode connections were further established for electrical operations [19,20]. Schematic of the cell is shown in Fig. 6. The fabricated cell has thickness of 2259 µm and the dimensions were 75 × 25 mm, shown in Fig. 7 (b), which is under experimental analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of parameters and description of the dataset
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of solar cells is characterized by the mentioned parameters; short-circuit current Isc, the open-circuit voltage VOC, and the fill factor FF which ultimately helps in determining the efficiency of the cell. Both the solar cells were placed under the sun for 16 days for a time period of 7 h on daily basis. The readings were taken after every 84 min, so for each day 5 readings were obtained. When the solar energy is available from the sun, the most significant value of current is measured for a cell. The current was measured with an ammeter and is known as the short circuit current of the solar cell. To measure the voltage of solar cells, a multimeter is used. The voltage so obtained is known as open-circuit voltage (VOC), this is the voltage of the unloaded circuit which is also the maximum voltage achieved by a solar cell. Analytically to determine these parameters, illuminated I-V characteristic is studied [21,22]. It is vital to perform the measurements under standard test conditions (STC) for a reliable measurement of the I-V characteristics. Also, the total irradiance should be equal to 1000 W/m2 for the solar cell. Further, the spectrum should resemble the AM 1.5 spectrum. Temperature is an important parameter in the performance of a solar cell which was measured using a thermometer. The average value of temperature measured for 16 days for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs was 32 °C and 34 °C respectively. The current which flows through the external circuit when the electrodes of the solar cell are short-circuited is known as the short-circuit current ISC. The photon flux incident on the solar cell is defined as the factor on which ISC is dependent. Flux is regulated by the spectral density of the incident light. The spectrum should be consistent with the AM 1.5 spectrum for standard solar cell measurements [23]. Hence it was observed that the value of spectral power density lies between 1005.6–1009.4 W/m2 depending on the average measured light intensity 2508 lx for 16 days for ZnO/GO cell. On the contrary the value of spectral power density for ZnO/CQDs cell lies between 1004.2–1006.5 W/m2 for an average value of 2612 lx (light intensity) for 16 days.

After obtaining experimental values of the vital parameters and verifying it with the earlier reported work, the photovoltaic cell so prepared was recapitulated by simulations for different values of temperature and spectral densities and, hence ISC, VOC, and FF of the cell along with the I-V characteristics were recorded. In total, 7458 iterations were run and 82,786 values were computed that were used to train the model for developing the dataset [24,25]. Tables 1a and 1b
consists of parameter settings and responses for the experimental run of ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs solar cell respectively which was recorded during the experimentation for 16 days. Further, this dataset was divided into certain proportions of Training, Testing, and Validation for the four output parameters, to extract the maximum value of output from the modeled ANN. To set up the data, all the values of input parameters were varied and the current and voltage values were calculated accordingly [26,27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Formulation of neural network for photovoltaic parameter optimization
ce:section.ce:para: The set of accessible data is distributed into three subsets: training, validation, and testing sets. The initial set of data is the training set followed by validation and testing sets. To provide input to the neural network for adaptation and learning, a training set has been used [28,29]. The inputs to the neural network are the spectral power densities of hybrid solar cells and the geographical temperature where it is installed. The maximum power outputs associated with each solar cell are termed as targets. During the training phase, the performance of the network is evaluated by the MSE (mean squared error), and the best experimental results are represented in Figs. 10(a–d) and 11(a–d) for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs respectively. MATLAB® (R2015a) was used to perform ANN modeling. Also, for the fitting of the curve and creation of a successful model of the network, the Neural Network Toolbox of MATLAB has been brought in use [30]. 7458 iterations were run and 82,786 values were computed that were used to train the model for developing the dataset. The percentage of data distribution under the three subsets for both the solar cells is shown in Table 2 . Different percentage of data was used during training, testing, and validation in order to generalize the experiment. For this type of data set we can conclude that any possible combination will give good result for this experiment [31,32].

Fig. 8 , shows the implemented Simulink diagram of the basic neural network. To train a multilayer feed-forward network, two inputs parameters (Spectral Power Density, Temperature), one hidden layer with 10 neurons and six output parameters i.e. Open circuit voltage (VOC), Variation in short circuit current (ISC), fill factor (FF), Maximum power prediction point (MPP), Incident Photon-to-electron Conversion Efficiency (IPCE), Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE) were recorded. In this investigation, the ideal condition of a solar cell has been considered i.e. shunt and series resistances have been taken as zero [33,34].

The scholastic meta-heuristic firefly algorithm is presented to identify the output parameters of the solar cell. In the firefly algorithm, the objective function of a given optimization problem is correlated with the flashing light [35]. Also, it is stated the swarm of fireflies is assisted by the light intensity in moving towards brighter and captivating sites to obtain optimal solutions for the photovoltaic parameters- Open circuit voltage (VOC), variation in short circuit current (ISC), fill factor (FF), Maximum power prediction point (MPP), Incident Photon-to-electron Conversion Efficiency (IPCE), Power Conversion Efficiency (PCE). The spectral density and temperature are the inputs and the current–voltage curve is measured with 80 points. The experimental points are obtained from standard tests on the fabricated cell at 35 °C. This algorithm has been used for training purposes as it is fast and provides results with the least errors. Its significant advantage is the use of primary real random numbers. The firefly algorithm functions on three particular rules. These are based on the key flashing attributes of real fireflies. These are as follows [36].
1)
Being unisex, all the fireflies’ moves towards more flashing ones regardless of their sex.
2)
Because air absorbs light, the brightness is proportional to the degree of attractiveness of a firefly. As the brightness decreases, the distance from the other firefly increases. The movement of fireflies will become aimless if it is unable to find another bright and more attractive fly.
3)
The brightness or light intensity of a firefly is determined by the value of the objective function of a given problem. For maximization problems, the light intensity is proportional to the value of the objective function.

ANN is trained in such a way that it reduces the error between the input parameters and the target to be achieved. Further, the weights and biases are adjusted to minimize the errors and to get the best possible value for the output parameters. A reliable network with the least error is obtained by dynamic updating of weights and biases. Here the utility function method or weighting function method is used to apply the firefly algorithm. It is a systematic method to deal with disagreement among the distinct goals. In this method, by using a utility function Ui fi for each objective function, the problem is transformed into a single-objective function. The overall utility function of the problem is defined as shown in Eq. (1) [35].

In particular, the firefly algorithm makes ANN more reliable by minimizing the error and enhances the performance of ANN. Ultimately the sum of the squared errors is penalized by this algorithm and a successful dynamic revision of weight assignment according to the magnitude of error occurred is performed [36]. Now after applying FFA for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs, the range of variation of the six output parameters is obtained, which is shown in Tables 3 and 4
respectively. Calculations of the same are reported in the appendix.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussions on use of firefly algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: Multilayer Feed Forward Neural Network consisting of one hidden layer (10 neurons) was instructed with the firefly algorithm on MATLAB (R2015a) software (system memory-16 GB). After training, the network, outcomes for each photovoltaic parameter were reported as per the information presented in Fig. 9 (a–d) for ZnO/GO cell and Fig. 10 (a–d) for ZnO/CQDs cell. Fig. 9(a–d) represents the value of the coefficient of correlation (R) during training, validation, testing, and an overall result for output 1, i.e. open-circuit voltage (VOC), output 2, i.e. Short circuit current (ISC), output 3, i.e. Fill factor (FF) and output 4, i.e. Voltage at maximum point (VMPP) respectively. The figures hereby indicate that the line passes linearly through almost 90% of the data and portrays a strong relationship among the points and hence desirable value of R is obtained for all the parameters of ZnO/GO cell.

Similarly, Fig. 10(a–d) represents the value of the coefficient of correlation (R) during training, validation, testing, and an overall result for output 1, i.e. open-circuit voltage (VOC), output 2, i.e. short circuit current (ISC), output 3, i.e. Fill factor (FF) and output 4, i.e. Voltage at maximum point (VMPP) respectively. Hence, the value of the regression model is close to 1 which suggests that it is structured well to give good results, also a relatable relationship has been developed between the input parameters and the target values [32]. The figures infer that values of R for all the estimated parameters get refined upon blending ZnO with CQDs. So it is valid to state that the performance of the ZnO/CQDs cell is better than ZnO/GO. Further performance graphs for a trained neural network for all four parameters of ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs is shown in Fig. 11 (a–d) and 12(a–d) respectively. The value of best validation performance is 0.00094782 at epoch 4. It is a low prediction error estimated with MSE as depicted in Fig. 11(a). The training was extended for 10 iterations till it got terminated. The figure also states that there is no major problem with the training. There is a slight difference in the test and validation curve.

The value for the best validation performance of Isc is 0.00028968 at epoch 2. A low prediction error has been traced with MSE shown in Fig. 11(b). The training was extended for 8 iterations till it ends up. The figure indicates that all the curves follow a similar trend.

For fill factor (FF), The value of best validation performance is 8.0343e-06 at epoch 3, shown in Fig. 11(c). It highlights the best validation performance concerning the iteration. The training was extended for 9 iterations till it got terminated. The figure indicates that a similar trend is followed by validation and test curves and no major issues were reported during training.

For VMPP, The value of best validation performance is 0.0083808 at epoch 1, shown in Fig. 11(d). The training was extended for 7 iterations till it got terminated. No major issues were reported during training. The figure shows that a slight difference in trend is followed by the validation and test curves.
For ZnO/CQDs, the best value of validation performance for Voc is 0.00056586 at epoch 9, measured with MSE shown in Fig. 12 (a).

For the iteration, it represents the best validation performance. Here the training was extended for 15 iterations till it comes to an end. The figure indicates a similar trend is followed by the validation and test curves.

For ISC, The best value of validation performance for Isc is 0.00027883 at epoch 6, shown in Fig. 12(b). The training was extended for 12 iterations till it terminates. The figure shows that no major problems with the training were reported and a similar trend is followed by the validation and test curves.

For FF, The best value of validation performance for FF is 3.8869e-05 at epoch 11, shown in Fig. 12(c). The training was extended for 17 iterations till it terminates. The figure indicates that no serious issue was reported with the training and a similar trend is followed by test and validation curves.

For VMPP, The best value of validation performance for VMPP is 0.00010272 at epoch 5, shown in Fig. 12(d). The training was extended for 11 iterations till it ends up. The figure indicates no issues with the training. Test curves and validation curves follow a slight difference in trend.
Fig. 13 shows the IV characteristics curve of the obtained results from the firefly algorithm for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs respectively.

For the trained network, the fit curve obtained by the ‘Plot fit’ function was used to record points of optimization generated by the ANN [35]. Table 5 , shows the comparison of the optimized points extracted from the two ANN models for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs cells.

It is observed that the points determined by both models have very little disagreement. After analyzing both the models, it is concluded that the results of the various parameters under observation for ZnO/CQDs are considerably better than the other one. The difference in the results is tabulated in Table 6 .

A comparative study is also performed on the changes in values of R for both the cells. It is deduced that significant improvement in the overall values of the coefficient of correlation is observed on the addition of CQDs. The tabulation of the same is represented in Table 6. The final comparison is made between the experimental treatment and the optimized treatment, shown in Table 7 . The values obtained by the experiment are in proper coordination with the ones validated by ANN. Tables 8 and 9
shows the comparison between the different techniques LMSA (Levenberg-Marquardt algorithm combined with Simulated Annealing), CARO (Chaotic Asexual Reproduction Optimization), NM-MPSO (hybrid Nelder-Mead and Modified Particle Swarm Optimization) used to obtain the six listed parameters for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs respectively [30,31,32,34]. So, we come to an inference that the fabricated cell is efficient enough to cater to the needs. Also, the advantage of using ANN is that it takes approximately 60 s to be trained to fetch a result whereas experimentation takes a comparatively longer period and more computational expense.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in solar trigeneration unit for commercial complex

ce:section.ce:para:
Solar trigeneration systems are able to simultaneously generate electricity, heat and cooling power from solar energy. For modelling and simulation purpose we have used Polysun 12.0.6 software by Vela Solaris AG, Winterthur, Switzerland. For designing, simulation and optimisation of comprehensive energy systems for residential as well as commercial buildings, Polysun software is a emerging viable option to serve the need appropriately. The vital aspect when planning an energy system is that it should works in consensus with the need and at the same time proves to be cost-effective. This software has an intelligent computational core which helps in realistically planning the energy system at a primary stage, thus providing genuine plans to the end users. Simulation of more than 150,000 components can be performed, based on different characteristic curves of various combinations of solar modules, further comparison and efficient optimisation can also be performed at the same platform, hence promising energy efficient and profitable system in a short span of time. The user is blessed with maximum flexibility along with vibrant new ideas to develop their own energy systems to meet the electrical demands [37,38,39]. A clear diagrammatic proposal of the two solar trigeneration systems developed in Polysun 12.0.6 is presented in Figs. 14a and 14b
) for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs modules respectively.

The main elements of the unit are PV modules, PV/T collectors, thermal storage tanks (TST) for DHW (DHW-TST) and HVAC (HVAC-TST) and an air-to-water vapor compression heat pump (HP). Application is well defined to model a simplest and authentic arrangement to cater the electrical needs of a commercial complex. Installation of HVAC- TST is optional. Although is provides increased flexibility but it ultimately increases initial costs, maintenance and installation space. The installed heat pump is suitable for operating at higher efficiency and also helps in storing hot and cold water in the HVAC-TST which can be later used. Electric power to the loads is provided by the PV modules and the PV/T collectors. To enable continous system operation load could be provided by a storage element or instantaneously. The solar DHW system is powered by the PV/T collectors along with a thermal storage tank, i.e. DHW-TST. Provision and extraction of heat to and from the building is headed by heat pump unit provides and extracts [40–42]. The energy flow of the ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs system is illustrated respectively in Figs. 15a and 15b
). The performance prediction of the trigeneration system is better understood by the system model. In the proposed trigeneration system, performance of the electric system depends on the quality of the components used and the place of installation. The combined performance of solar collectors and thermal storage tanks is also considered as it is influenced by transient dynamics [43–45]. For developing the system model Polysun 12.0.6 simulation package has been used. The model integrates all the system components which were already available in the software library. The climate model uses input data from the wizard of present at graphical user interface, which is repository of free climate data for building performance simulation.

Polysun simulation software allows making a single line diagram for residential and commercial Energy Efficient Building. System overview, Overview solar thermal energy, electricity consumption, heat pump running status, different component result analysis and annual energy flow diagram has also been studied by the software. These values are calculated on annual basis, the software generated results are attached in the appendix. Electric load profiles were concerned with domestic appliances, lighting, thermal (HP and DHW load profiles). The trigeneration system model is formed by proper selection of device parameters obtained by optimized results from firefly algorithm, hence two different systems were modeled to study the application of the hybrid solar cells [46].

The proposed trigeneration systems were simulated for a typical meteorological year (TMY) under Asian climatic data. The actual simulated setup is presented in Figs. 14a and 14b) for ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs modules respectively. The system is simulated with all the essential components to provide results against which improvements in fabrication, modelling, simulation and actual experimentation can be evaluated. Tables 8 and 9 elaborates the details of the modelled system of ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs in a tabular comparative form to make it easy to understand.

Haldia is located at Longitude: 88.01° and Latitude: 21.94°. The climate is considered to be has a tropical savanna climate. with winter temperatures ranging from a low of around 7 °C (45 °F) degrees Celsius to a high of 22 °C (72 °F). As per the meterological data, Average outdoor temperature was 26.4 °C, Annual global irradiation was 727 kWh/m2 and annual diffuse irradiation was 894 kWh/m2. Simulations were conducted on North Service Centre (Olefin building) of Haldia Petrochemicals Limited, Haldia, West Bengal, an office complex, whose construction elements are in accordance with Indian building codes, for 35 occupants with 2 floors and Heated/air-conditioned living area was 3,300 m2.

The ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs constituted PV modules used for this simulation are each 25 in number, both having Collector area of 68 m2 single cover sheet and tube collectors whose total electricity production was 35,451 kWh and 35,416 kWh respectively, maximum energy savings were 26,683.6 kWh and 26,277.1 kWh respectively. The solar DHW system for ZnO/GO based unit was simulated with 32 m2 of PV/T collector area, Inflow temperature during operation 41.3 °C, Outflow temperature during operation 37.6 °C respectively; other properties of flat plate PV/T collector are specified in Table 10 . Regarding the HVAC system, a commercial air-to-water HP unit with adequate power rating was selected and set to produce cold and hot water respectively. Results for the heating and cooling elements incorporated in the system are listed in Table 11 . Selection of fan-coils has been made for simulation of terminal HVAC units [47–49]. The number of PV modules installed and the position of slope angle at for purpose of simulation can be user defined and also determined by the software [50]. According to the simulation results, the annual production and demand for the two proposed trigeneration systems is represented in Table 12 . A comparative study is made between the two systems and it was concluded that the ZnO/CQDs simulated unit is better than ZnO/GO unit. Heat Pump for ZnO/GO unit at which it operates is 76.7% and ZnO/CQDs unit heat pump has efficiency of 76.4%.

The solar DHW system of ZnO/GO unit performs well, reaching a solar thermal fraction of 82.7%, Solar fraction (hot water) of 82.9%, Solar fraction (building) of 82.9%. For ZnO/CQDs unit solar thermal fraction was 84%, values for solar fraction (hot water) was 84.1% and solar fraction (building) was 84.1%. DC and AC energy produced by the PV/T collectors was 6,438 kWh and 5,873 kWh respectively.

This study was based on the synthesis, optimization, and validation of the two-hybrid solar cells i.e. ZnO/GO and ZnO/CQDs. In this work, significance for the optimization of a hybrid solar cell is discussed. For the optimization, modeling was done on the data obtained for the two cells using Artificial Neural Network. MATLAB (2015Ra) was used to develop a network with 10 hidden neurons, which was trained using the firefly algorithm through 7458 iterations which were run and 82,786 values were determined, that were further used to train the neural network model for the generation of the dataset. The generation of the most optimum model of the two solar cells was achieved by using the best value of the parameters predicted by the trained network. The results obtained by the model were compared with the experimental values. It is deduced that there is a fine consensus between the two modes of obtaining the results. The optimized values of VOC, ISC, FF, VMPP, IPCE, PCE for ZnO/GO are 0.623 V, 0.546 mA, 62%, 0.509 V, 36.95%, 9.12% respectively. Similarly values of VOC, ISC, FF, VMPP, IPCE, PCE for ZnO/CQDs are 0.636 V, 0.597 mA, 68%, 0.531 V, 40.72%, 10.35% respectively. Also, value of the coefficient of correlation (R) obtained is close to 1, which means ANN/FA has proved the validity of the experiment. As the results obtained are good, so we can say that the experiments were conducted in sound line. The working efficiency of the CQDs cell is better than the GO cell. Further, the trial of the values so obtained was done. The I-V characteristic showed that the firefly algorithm functioned efficiently. It also suggests the capability of predicting highly optimized and useful hybrid solar cells hence making it a profitable asset to society. One of the applications of these cells have been proposed in modeling and simulation of trigeneration system based on PV/T collectors, PV modules and a Heat Pump. The designing of the system was made easy by using Polysun simulation software version 12.0.6 but the fabrication process is bit expensive. The selection was made on traditional system results from where domestic load profile was selected, i.e. grid-connected, load profiles. Also, it is observed that both the system models validates an intensive understanding regarding the drawbacks of solar technology i.e. if the demand soars high in low irradiation months, then these simulated systems helps predict the energy usage as well as the performance during windy and cold weather, hence suggesting the seasonal energy storage requirement and need for alternative energy source during winter seasons. The nucleus of the subsequent research will be on enhancing the present system model and setting up comprehensive cost theories to avoid unproductive events and verify designs of cost-effective systems.

Sakshi Tyagi: Conceptualization, Writing - original draft, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Pawan Kumar Singh: Data curation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Arun Kumar Tiwari: Validation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Pritam Pain: Visualization, Software, Writing - review & editing.

We are grateful for his help and constant support during the research propagation.
